questions.prompt: What are the two related mechanisms an attacker can use to exfiltrate mail from a compromised mailbox?
choices: Inbox rules (mailbox-level rules acting on incoming mail) and mailbox forwarders (ForwardingSmtpAddress / ForwardingAddress).

choices.label: Inbox rules and Conditional Access policies.
choices.rationale: Conditional Access is an admin-side control, not an attacker exfiltration mechanism.

choices.label: Sent-items archiving and SharePoint sharing.
choices.rationale: Neither is the lesson's named pair.

choices.label: Calendar delegation and Teams forwarding.
choices.rationale: Not the mail-exfiltration mechanism the lesson describes.

questions.explanation: Two paths can be set up together. Removing one leaves the other.

questions.prompt: Why do attackers route mail to folders named '.', 'RSS Subscriptions', or 'Conversation History'?

choices.label: Those folders archive faster and the attacker wants the mail to disappear.
choices.rationale: Not how Outlook archival works.

choices: Hidden or rarely-used folders mean the legitimate user does not see replies to phishing the attacker sent from their mailbox.

choices.label: Those folders are required for inbox-rule filtering to work.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the platform behaviour.

choices.label: Those folders bypass Conditional Access policies.
choices.rationale: Folders do not interact with Conditional Access.

questions.explanation: The attacker hides their tracks so the user does not notice the compromise activity.

questions.prompt: "Removing the inbox rule is enough." Why is this often wrong?

choices.label: Inbox rules cannot be removed without senior approval.
choices.rationale: Rule removal is in the helpdesk's playbook scope.

choices.label: Removing rules requires a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Rule removal is a portal/admin-tool action.

choices: The attacker may also have configured a mailbox-level forwarder. Same attack, two mechanisms. Removing only one leaves exfiltration intact.

choices.label: Rules re-create themselves automatically when removed.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Check for ForwardingSmtpAddress separately. The lesson's central discipline: do not remove one of two mechanisms.

questions.prompt: Which inbox-rule pattern is MOST consistent with attacker behaviour per the lesson?

choices.label: Rule marks mail from the user's manager as high-priority.
choices.rationale: User-organisation pattern, not attacker-shaped.

choices.label: Rule moves all newsletters to a 'Reading' folder.
choices.rationale: Personal organisation pattern, not attacker-shaped.

choices.label: Rule moves project mail from one client into a project folder.
choices.rationale: Routine power-user routing, not attacker-shaped.

choices: Rule forwards mail with 'invoice', 'payment', or 'wire' keywords to an external address and deletes or moves the original.
questions.explanation: Forward and hide on financial keywords is the classic compromise shape.

questions.prompt: An inbox rule moves mail from security@microsoft.com to 'Conversation History' and marks as read. What is the attacker intent?
choices: Suppress security notifications so the user does not see 'new sign-in detected' alerts.

choices.label: Reduce mailbox clutter.
choices.rationale: Mailbox-organisation framing for an obvious attacker intent.

choices.label: Comply with retention policy.
choices.rationale: Retention policies are not user-rule territory.

choices.label: Speed up mail processing.
choices.rationale: Marking as read does not speed up processing. It hides notifications.

questions.explanation: Compromise-pattern by intent: hide the security alerts so the user does not notice the attacker's activity.

questions.prompt: You find ForwardingSmtpAddress set to the same attacker domain. The Recommendation did not mention removing the forwarder. What do you do?

choices.label: Remove the forwarder yourself since it is obviously malicious.
choices.rationale: Acting on context the Recommendation did not include. The finding goes back to the SOC.

choices.label: Wait for the SOC to update the Recommendation before doing anything.
choices.rationale: The named rule is exfiltrating now. Act on the named actions while surfacing the additional finding.

choices: Execute the Recommendation as written, and in parallel reply to the SOC asking whether the forwarder should be removed.
questions.explanation: Execute the Recommendation cleanly. Surface the additional finding to the layer that decides.

questions.prompt: Why is DeliverToMailboxAndForward: true a particularly effective attacker setting?

choices.label: It locks the user out of their own mailbox.
choices.rationale: The user keeps their mailbox. The attacker gets a copy.

choices.label: It bypasses MFA.
choices.rationale: MFA is not involved in mail forwarding.

choices: The user still sees their mail in their inbox and does not notice, while the attacker receives a copy externally.

choices.label: It deletes the user's existing mail.
choices.rationale: No deletion occurs. The user sees everything.

questions.explanation: Stealth-by-design: the user sees nothing missing, while the attacker has a copy of everything.

questions.prompt: "If the user is the one who created the rule, it must be legitimate." Why is this wrong?

choices.label: Users cannot create inbox rules without admin approval.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the platform behaviour.

choices.label: Inbox rules are always created by attackers.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Most rules in most tenants are legitimate.

choices.label: All inbox rules are malicious by default.
choices.rationale: Overcorrects. Many legitimate rules exist.

choices: Not if the user's account was compromised at the time. The Timeline tells you whether the rule creation correlates with a suspicious sign-in.
questions.explanation: The Timeline view discriminates. Rule creation during a compromise window is attacker activity wearing the user's identity.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a 'weird but legitimate' inbox-rule pattern per the lesson?

choices.label: A rule that forwards *invoice* mail to an external Gmail address and deletes originals.
choices.rationale: That is the compromise pattern.

choices: A user forwarding their work mail to a personal address for weekend reading.

choices.label: A rule that moves security@microsoft.com mail to a hidden folder.
choices.rationale: That is the suppress-notifications attacker intent.

choices.label: A rule that sends a fake out-of-office to specific contacts.
choices.rationale: That is the social-engineering attacker pattern.

questions.explanation: Often against IT policy but not attacker-shaped. The SOC weighs context. The Recommendation tells you which case this is.

questions.prompt: What do you check before declaring the inbox-rule playbook complete?

choices.label: Whether the destination email is also used elsewhere in the mailbox.
choices.rationale: Defensible but secondary. The check the lesson centres on is different.

choices: Whether the mailbox also has ForwardingSmtpAddress or ForwardingAddress set, since the second mechanism may be present alongside the rule.

choices.label: Skip any pre-check; execute exactly as written.
choices.rationale: Execute the Recommendation, yes. The check for an additional forwarder is what makes the response complete.

questions.explanation: The lesson's central discipline: do not remove one of two mechanisms. Check for both.
